race.name: THE MAJESTICS (formerly UCI Gravel Suisse)
race.tagline: UCI World Series gravel in the Swiss Alps — where the world cycling headquarters meets alpine climbing

vitals.date_specific: 2027: July 11 (Sunday, PATTERN ESTIMATE — second Sunday of July, per 2026 date; official 2027 date not yet announced); 2026 edition (Sunday July 12, Aigle start) completed CONFIRMED, now historical

logistics.explanation: International travel to Switzerland required. Geneva airport is closest but still requires train or car rental. Swiss hotels are expensive, and getting bikes through European travel adds complexity. Worth it, but takes planning.

length.explanation: At 106km, the granfondo sits solidly in mid-range territory - substantial but not ultra distance. The 72km mediofondo provides a shorter alternative. Elite rider Tessa Neefjes emphasizes preparation: "I like to arrive early and do proper recons. I study the climbs, the downhills, the gravel and the lines." Similar distance to Crusher in the Tushar.

technicality.explanation: Described as "pure gravel" that keeps riders "really in nature, with no traffic, proper gravel climbs and technical descents." The course is "constantly up and down" with technical, fast gravel downhills. Less tarmac than some UCI gravel events that "feel more like road races." Similar technical demands to Gravel Worlds.

elevation.explanation: 2,500 meters (8,200 feet) of climbing over 106km makes this one of the more vertical races on the UCI calendar, even after the course was revised down from an earlier ~3,440m/110km configuration. Real climbing runs through the Route des Chaux area en route to Col de la Croix — alpine terrain with sustained gradient rather than flat gravel grinding. Comparable in spirit to Badlands, though less extreme than the pre-revision course.

climate.explanation: Historical weather data shows average highs of 63°F and lows of 46°F, with a 78% chance of rain on any given day. Alpine conditions in July can be deceptively harsh - cool temperatures at altitude, rapidly changing mountain weather, potential for afternoon storms. Finish at Col de la Croix at 1,819m can get cold even in summer. Milder conditions than Crusher in the Tushar.

altitude.explanation: Start at valley level (Aigle, ~380m) then climb to Col de la Croix (1,819m) before finishing near Villars-sur-Ollon. Moderate altitude impact - noticeable for flatlanders but not the oxygen debt you'd get at 10,000ft+. Similar altitude considerations to Grinduro.

adventure.explanation: Starting at the UCI World Cycling Center headquarters then climbing through Vaudois Alps forests and ridges to finish at Col de la Croix. This venue has been awarded the 2029 UCI Gravel World Championships - bucket-list territory for any cycling purist.

prestige.explanation: Part of the UCI Gravel World Series with direct qualification to World Championships. The venue has been awarded the 2029 UCI Gravel World Championships, making this as prestigious as gravel racing gets outside of Unbound. Elite field includes Andreas Seewald and Sophie Wright.

race_quality.explanation: Start area relocates to the UCI headquarters in Aigle creating a unique setting. Swiss precision meets UCI standards with feed zones every 25-30km, full mechanical and medical support, proper timing chips, and characteristic Swiss attention to detail.

experience.explanation: Tessa Neefjes captures the atmosphere: "It feels like a festival before and after the race... You finish and there are happy people everywhere. You can sit on the grass, eat, drink, relax. It feels like a celebration." Rolling out from UCI headquarters adds gravitas.

community.explanation: Growing festival atmosphere with cross-community connection. Tessa Neefjes notes: "After the race, you can talk with amateur riders. You've all ridden the same course, so you can really share the experience." Still building identity as newer UCI event (first edition 2024).

field_depth.explanation: UCI World Series status attracts strong European pros - Andreas Seewald, Sophie Wright, and former Unbound winner Carolin Schiff have all competed. Not major series depth, but serious international competition with World Championship qualification on the line. Deeper field than most — comparable to Badlands. As Tessa Neefjes puts it: "I like to arrive early and do proper recons. I study the climbs, the downhills, the gravel and the lines. I want to know where time can be gained and where attacks might happen."

value.explanation: Entry fees are reasonable by European UCI standards with Swiss Cycling member discounts (20%) and #fastandfemaleSUI discounts (15%) available. Solid included amenities and fair value for a UCI qualifier, though Switzerland is never cheap overall.

expenses.explanation: Switzerland will destroy your wallet. Hotels range $186-304/night, flights from North America run $1,000+, Swiss food and transport are expensive by any standard. Easily a $2,500-3,500 trip for Americans. Europeans have it easier, but still premium pricing. More expensive than Atlas Mountain Race.

date.typical: Second weekend of July (multi-discipline: TT Friday, Road Saturday, Gravel Sunday)
date.year2027_estimate: July 11, 2027 (Sunday, PATTERN ESTIMATE — not yet officially announced)

distances.name: Granfondo ("The Summit")
distances.description: Full UCI qualifying distance for all age groups - from UCI headquarters in Aigle to the Villars-sur-Ollon area via Col de la Croix

distances.name: Mediofondo ("The Ridge")
distances.description: Official qualifying distance for men 60+ and women 50+ age groups

terrain.overview: Roughly half paved / half unpaved alpine terrain (organizer figure not published to the mile for the current course; treat as approximate). Starts flat in the Rhone Valley from Aigle, transitions to forest climbs and high-altitude gravel roads through the Route des Chaux area en route to Col de la Croix, finishing near Villars-sur-Ollon.

registration.notes: Early registration discounts available. Swiss Cycling members receive 20% discount.

race.weather: Alpine summer conditions - generally pleasant but highly variable. Cool at altitude, potential for afternoon storms. Temperature can drop significantly at the Col de la Croix finish (1,819m).
uniqueCharacteristics: Starts at UCI World Cycling Center headquarters in Aigle - the global home of cycling
Host venue for 2029 UCI Gravel World Championships
Part of UCI Gravel World Series with Worlds qualification
Rebranded 'The Majestics' (merger of UCI Gran Fondo Suisse + UCI Gravel Suisse, first run as 'Gravel Suisse' in 2024)
Multi-discipline weekend: TT Friday, Road Saturday, Gravel Sunday
Route through Route des Chaux to Col de la Croix, finishing near Villars-sur-Ollon
race.popularity: High - premier Swiss gravel event and UCI World Series qualifier

final_verdict.one_liner: The UCI's backyard playground where 2,500 meters of Alpine climbing separates tourists from gravel racers.
final_verdict.should_you_race: You need legitimate climbing legs and a gravel bike that can handle steep alpine gradients on loose surfaces. The Route des Chaux/Col de la Croix section destroys weekend warriors—if you can't sustain power on steep off-road climbs above 1,500 meters, it will expose you. Come for the prestige and alpine scenery, but expect to earn every meter of that Col de la Croix summit.
final_verdict.alternatives: Unbound 200 offers similar suffering with less elevation but more distance and heat. Traka 360 in Spain delivers comparable climbing with better weather windows and fewer altitude issues for sea-level dwellers.

course_description.character: Starting at cycling's global headquarters in Aigle, this course runs through Rhone Valley flats before climbing through forest tracks and the Route des Chaux area to Col de la Croix (1,819m), finishing near Villars-sur-Ollon. Revised down from an earlier ~110km/3,440m configuration to the current ~106km/2,500m Granfondo — still a serious alpine test with a UCI stamp.

suffering_zones.label: Ollon Wake-Up Call
suffering_zones.desc: Early off-road climbing tests legs that haven't warmed up yet, per organizer/rider course notes.

suffering_zones.label: Rhone Valley
suffering_zones.desc: Flatter roads through the valley before the terrain turns upward — the calm before the Route des Chaux climbing.

suffering_zones.label: Route des Chaux
suffering_zones.desc: Forest gravel climbing through the Chaux/Gryon ski-area terrain, the main climbing block en route to Col de la Croix.

suffering_zones.label: Col de la Croix Summit
suffering_zones.desc: The high point of the course (1,819m) before the descent into the Villars-sur-Ollon finish.

course_description.signature_challenge: The Route des Chaux-to-Col de la Croix block is the defining test: alpine forest gravel climbing at altitude, arriving after Rhone Valley miles have already put time in riders' legs.
course_description.ridewithgps_name: 2025-era 'The Summit' route archived (110km/3,440m, superseded by the current ~106km/2,500m Majestics course); current GPX distributed via official event registration/finishers.com

primary.name: Specialized Pathfinder Pro
primary.why: Fast center, grippy edges. The modern all-rounder benchmark.

front_rear_split.rationale: Faster front for reduced rolling resistance on straights, grippier rear for traction on loose climbs and corners

migration_notes: 2026-07-16 P1 fact base: RENAME CANDIDATE flagged for Matti — official event has rebranded 'The Majestics' (merger of UCI Gran Fondo Suisse + UCI Gravel Suisse; first run as 'Gravel Suisse' in 2024). Facts in this profile reconciled under the current identity; slug (gravel-fondo-switzerland) and site copy untouched pending Matti's naming decision. Course also revised down from ~110km/3,440m to current ~106km/2,500m (Granfondo/'The Summit') and ~72km/2,000m (Mediofondo/'The Ridge'); next-edition date is a Sunday July 11, 2027 pattern estimate (2026 was Sunday July 12, official 2027 date not yet announced).
